title: Subjunctive Triggers: the WEIRDO and beyond
description: Lesson 1 built every form; now learn WHEN to use the subjunctive. The trigger families (Wishes, Emotions, Impersonal expressions, Recommendations, Doubt/denial, Ojalá), the conjunctions of time and purpose (cuando, para que, antes de que), and relative clauses with an unknown antecedent. All hinge on 'que' + a change of subject.

cards.front: Quiero que vengas. / Espero que llames.
cards.back: wishes, wants and requests trigger the subjunctive
cards.notes: Verbs of **wanting, hoping and requesting** take *que* + subjunctive when the subject changes: *querer que*, *esperar que*, *pedir que*, *preferir que*. *Quiero que **vengas*** (I want you to come). Same subject? Use the infinitive: *Quiero **venir***.

cards.front: Me alegro de que estés aquí. / Es una pena que te vayas.
cards.back: emotions and value judgments trigger the subjunctive
cards.notes: Reactions and feelings about something take the subjunctive: *alegrarse de que*, *sentir que* (to be sorry that), *tener miedo de que*, *es una pena que*, *me gusta que*. *Me alegro de que **estés** aquí* (I'm glad you're here).

cards.front: Es importante que estudies. / Es necesario que vengas.
cards.notes: *Es importante/necesario/posible/mejor que* + subjunctive. BUT expressions that **assert a fact** stay in the indicative: *es verdad que*, *es obvio que*, *es cierto que*. Contrast: *Es posible que **venga*** vs. *Es verdad que **viene***.

cards.back: advice and influence trigger the subjunctive
cards.notes: Verbs that try to **influence** another person's behaviour take the subjunctive: *recomendar que*, *aconsejar que*, *sugerir que*, *es necesario que*. *Te aconsejo que **lo pienses*** (I advise you to think about it).

cards.front: No creo que venga. / Dudo que sea cierto.
cards.back: doubt and denial trigger the subjunctive
cards.notes: Doubt or denial → subjunctive: *no creer que*, *dudar que*, *no pensar que*, *no es verdad que*. The catch: the **affirmative** *creo que* / *pienso que* asserts a belief and takes the **indicative**. *Creo que **viene*** vs. *No creo que **venga***.

*Ojalá* ("I wish / let's hope") **always** triggers the subjunctive, with or without *que*: *Ojalá **gane** nuestro equipo* (Let's hope our team wins). Present subjunctive for a real hope; imperfect subjunctive for something unlikely.

cards.front: Cuando sea mayor, viajaré. / Cuando soy mayor, viajo. (habitual)
cards.back: cuando + subjunctive for the unrealised future; + indicative for habits
cards.notes: After **cuando, en cuanto, hasta que** use the subjunctive when the action is still **future/unrealised**: *Cuando **sea** mayor...* (When I'm older...). Use the indicative for habits or past facts: *Cuando **soy** mayor, me canso* (whenever / generally).

cards.front: antes de que llegues; para que entiendas; a menos que vengas
cards.back: some conjunctions ALWAYS take the subjunctive
cards.notes: These conjunctions take the subjunctive **every time**, because they introduce something not (yet) real: *antes de que* (before), *para que* (so that), *a menos que* (unless), *sin que*, *con tal de que*. *Te lo explico **para que** lo **entiendas***.

cards.front: Aunque llueva, saldremos. (even if) / Aunque llueve, salgo. (even though)
cards.back: aunque + subjunctive = even if (hypothetical); + indicative = even though (fact)
cards.notes: *Aunque* takes the **subjunctive** when the situation is hypothetical ("even if"): *Aunque **llueva**, saldremos* (Even if it rains...). It takes the **indicative** for a known fact ("even though"): *Aunque **llueve**, salgo* (Even though it's raining...).

cards.front: Busco a alguien que hable francés. / No hay nadie que sepa.
cards.back: relative clauses with an unknown or nonexistent antecedent take the subjunctive
cards.notes: When the thing or person you describe is **not known to exist** (or doesn't exist), the relative clause uses the subjunctive: *Busco a alguien que **hable** francés* (someone who may or may not exist). If it's a specific, real person → indicative: *Conozco a alguien que **habla** francés*. *No hay nadie que **sepa*** (nobody exists who knows).

cards.front: Quiero estudiar. (no change) / Quiero que estudies. (change)
cards.back: no change of subject → infinitive, not que + subjunctive
cards.notes: The subjunctive needs **two different subjects** across *que*. If the subject is the same, drop *que* and use the **infinitive**: *Quiero **estudiar*** (I want to study), *Espero **verte*** (I hope to see you). Change of subject → *Quiero que **estudies***.

cards.front: WEIRDO: Wishes, Emotions, Impersonal, Recommendations, Doubt, Ojalá
cards.back: a memory hook for the subjunctive trigger families
cards.notes: **WEIRDO** collects the main triggers: **W**ishes, **E**motions, **I**mpersonal expressions, **R**ecommendations/requests, **D**oubt/denial, **O**jalá. Add the time/purpose conjunctions and unknown-antecedent relatives, and you have the whole map.

steps.title: The WEIRDO map of triggers
steps.body:
# When do I actually use the subjunctive?

Lesson 1 built every form. The real skill is knowing **when** the subjunctive is required. Almost every case shares one shape:

> **main clause + que + [different subject] + subjunctive**

A handy memory hook is **WEIRDO**:

| Letter | Family | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **W** | Wishes / wants / requests | *Quiero que **vengas*** |
| **E** | Emotions / value judgments | *Me alegro de que **estés** aquí* |
| **I** | Impersonal expressions | *Es importante que **estudies*** |
| **R** | Recommendations / influence | *Te aconsejo que **descanses*** |
| **D** | Doubt / denial | *Dudo que **sea** cierto* |
| **O** | Ojalá | *Ojalá **llueva*** |

## The one big condition: change of subject

The subjunctive needs **two different subjects** joined by *que*.

- Same subject → **infinitive**: *Quiero **estudiar*** (I want to study).
- Different subject → **que + subjunctive**: *Quiero que **estudies*** (I want *you* to study).

English hides this with an infinitive (*I want you to study*), so never translate *to* literally.

steps.title: The families up close (and the indicative exceptions)
steps.body:
## Wishes, emotions, recommendations

These always trigger the subjunctive with a change of subject:

- **Wishes:** *querer que, esperar que, pedir que, preferir que*
- **Emotions:** *alegrarse de que, sentir que, tener miedo de que, es una pena que, me gusta que*
- **Recommendations:** *recomendar que, aconsejar que, sugerir que*

## Impersonal expressions — but watch the fact-asserting ones

Most take the subjunctive:

- *Es importante / necesario / posible / mejor que* + **subjunctive** → *Es posible que **venga***

But expressions that **state a fact** take the **indicative**:

- *Es verdad que, es obvio que, es cierto que* + **indicative** → *Es verdad que **viene***

## Doubt and denial — and the affirmative trap

| Expression | Mood | Example |
|---|---|---|
| *creo que / pienso que* | **indicative** | *Creo que **viene*** |
| *no creo que / no pienso que* | **subjunctive** | *No creo que **venga*** |
| *dudar que* | **subjunctive** | *Dudo que **sea** cierto* |
| *no es verdad que* | **subjunctive** | *No es verdad que **venga*** |

> The most common English-speaker error: *Creo que **venga***. Wrong! An affirmative belief is asserted → indicative: *Creo que **viene***.

steps.title: Conjunctions and unknown antecedents
steps.body:
## Time conjunctions: future vs. habit

After **cuando, en cuanto, hasta que**, choose the mood by whether the action is real yet:

- **Future / unrealised → subjunctive:** *Cuando **sea** mayor, viajaré* (When I'm older...).
- **Habit or past fact → indicative:** *Cuando **soy** mayor me canso* / *Cuando **era** niño, jugaba*.

> English uses the present ("when I **am** older") for the future, which tempts you toward the indicative. In Spanish it must be the subjunctive: *cuando **sea** mayor*.

## Conjunctions that ALWAYS take the subjunctive

These introduce something not (yet) real, so the subjunctive is automatic:

- *antes de que* (before) · *para que* (so that) · *a menos que* (unless) · *sin que* · *con tal de que*
- *Te lo explico **para que** lo **entiendas***.

## Aunque: even if vs. even though

- **Hypothetical (even if) → subjunctive:** *Aunque **llueva**, saldremos*.
- **Known fact (even though) → indicative:** *Aunque **llueve**, salgo*.

## Relative clauses with an unknown antecedent

If the person/thing described **may not exist**, the relative clause is subjunctive:

- *Busco a alguien que **hable** francés* (anyone who can) → subjunctive
- *Conozco a alguien que **habla** francés* (a specific person) → indicative
- *No hay nadie que **sepa** la respuesta* (nonexistent) → subjunctive

steps.title: Match the trigger to its family

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish trigger with its WEIRDO family.

steps.title: A wish with a change of subject

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I want you to call me tonight.* (querer que + subjunctive, tú)
accept: Quiero que me llames esta noche.
quiero que me llames esta noche
Quiero que me llames esta noche
Quiero que me llames hoy por la noche.
exercise.hint: querer que + subjunctive; llamar -> llames (tú). Different subjects, so use que, not the infinitive.
distractors: Quiero que me llamas esta noche.

exercise.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'I don't think it's true'? (doubt -> subjunctive)
exercise.hint: no creer que = doubt -> subjunctive; ser -> sea. Note that affirmative 'creo que' would take the indicative.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the subjunctive of *ser* (yo). 'When I am older, I will travel.'
exercise.sentence: Cuando ___ mayor, viajaré por todo el mundo.

accept: sea

exercise.hint: The action is still future/unrealised, so cuando takes the subjunctive: ser (yo) -> sea. English 'when I am' is misleading here.

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *I'll explain it to you so that you understand.*
exercise.hint: para que ALWAYS takes the subjunctive; entender -> entiendas (tú).

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I'm glad (that) you are here.* (alegrarse de que + subjunctive, estar)
accept: Me alegro de que estés aquí.
me alegro de que estés aquí
Me alegro de que estes aqui.
Me alegro que estés aquí.
Me alegro de que estés aquí
exercise.hint: alegrarse de que + subjunctive; estar -> estés (tú).
distractors: Me alegro de que estás aquí.
Me alegro de que seas aquí.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the subjunctive of *salir* (nosotros). 'before we leave'
exercise.sentence: Termina la tarea antes de que ___ de casa.

accept: salgamos

exercise.hint: antes de que ALWAYS takes the subjunctive; salir (salgo) -> salgamos (nosotros).

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I recommend that you rest more.* (recomendar que + subjunctive, tú)
accept: Te recomiendo que descanses más.
te recomiendo que descanses más
Te recomiendo que descanses mas.
Te recomiendo que descanses más

exercise.prompt: Match each trigger with the mood it requires.
Translate: *I'm looking for someone who speaks French.* (unknown person -> subjunctive)
accept: Busco a alguien que hable francés.
busco a alguien que hable francés
Busco a alguien que hable frances.
Busco a alguien que hable francés
exercise.hint: The person may not exist, so the relative clause is subjunctive; hablar -> hable. Compare: 'Conozco a alguien que habla francés' (real -> indicative).
distractors: Busco a alguien que habla francés.
Busco a alguien que hablar francés.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the subjunctive of *llover*. 'Even if it rains, we'll go out.'
exercise.sentence: Aunque ___ mañana, saldremos a la calle.

accept: llueva

exercise.hint: Here 'even if' is hypothetical -> subjunctive; llover (o->ue) -> llueva. (If it were a fact, 'even though', you'd use the indicative llueve.)

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words: *Let's hope our team wins tomorrow.* (Ojalá + subjunctive)
exercise.hint: Ojalá ALWAYS takes the subjunctive; ganar -> gane.
